rulings.Case Title: SABRINA MAREZ VS. LYFT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Lyft, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication As To Plaintiff Sabrina Marez'S Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 10, 2018, Line 4, Defendant Lyft, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication As To Plaintiff Sabrina Marez'S Complaint. Defendant Lyft, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment as to plaintiff Sabrina Marez's complaint is granted. After review of the supplemental papers filed by the parties, the court remains of the view that Le Elder v. Rice (1994) 21 Cal.App.4th 1604 controls this case and requires granting of Lyft's motion. While Yamaguchi v. Harnsmut (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 472 and Rodgers v. Kemper Constr. Co. (1975) 50 Cal.App.3d 608 both stand for the proposition that there can be a triable dispute regarding scope of employment when torts occur on an employer's real property outside of business hours, the circumstances here are more analogous to Le Elder. Notably, no case cited by counsel or disclosed by the court's own independent research states or suggests that liability can be imposed on an entity like Lyft based on a "the business is the car" rationale. Le Elder has been cited approvingly numerous times and most recently in 2013 and has never been criticized or overruled, remains good law, and is on-point authority requiring the granting of this motion. The "coming and going" rule does not apply because defendant Jonathan Gaurano was returning home from working at a conference unrelated to Lyft when the accident happened and had not activated the Lyft platform at any point that day. Because the coming and going rule is inapplicable, per Le Elder, this court may not employ a "benefit to the employer" analysis, which is urged by both plaintiffs. (Le Elder, 21 Cal.App.4th at 1608 ("The trial court mistakenly applied a 'benefit to the employer' analysis. It may have been referring to the benefit-to-the-employer exception to the 'coming and going rule, but that rule is inapt here.")(citation omitted)). As with the employee in Le Elder, Mr. Gaurano was engaged in a "purely personal" matter at the time the accident occurred. Mr. Gaurano was returning home to eat after working at a conference unrelated to Lyft and the employee in Le Elder was returning home to eat after dropping his kids off at school. (Id.) The primary consideration is not whether the vehicle can be fairly considered a company vehicle or whether Lyft benefitted from Mr. Gaurano driving it for personal reasons, but whether Mr. Gaurano was acting within the scope of the alleged employment, agency agreement, or joint venture when the accident happened. (Id. at 1609 ("The existence of nonexistence of employer benefits is not dispositive in determining vicarious liability. Rather, the dispositive factor is whether an employee was acting within the scope of employment when the tort was committed.") (citation omitted)). None of the cases relied on by the plaintiffs are to the contrary. Lazar v. Thermal Equip. Corp. (1983) 148 Cal.App.3d 458, 463, 465-67 applied an exception to the coming and going rule applied because the driver's deviation from the scope of employment was minimal. Sumrall v. Modern Alloys, Inc. (2017) 10 Cal.App.5th 961 reversed a trial court's grant of summary judgment because it was a question of fact whether the driver was on a "business errand" when the accident occurred. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: SABRINA MAREZ VS. LYFT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Lyft, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication As To Plaintiff Marissa Cruz'S Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 10, 2018, Line 4, Defendant Lyft, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication As To Plaintiff Marissa Cruz'S Complaint. Defendant Lyft, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment as to plaintiff Marissa Cruz's complaint is granted. After review of the supplemental papers filed by the parties, the court remains of the view that Le Elder v. Rice (1994) 21 Cal.App.4th 1604 controls this case and requires granting of Lyft's motion. While Yamaguchi v. Harnsmut (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 472 and Rodgers v. Kemper Constr. Co. (1975) 50 Cal.App.3d 608 both stand for the proposition that there can be a triable dispute regarding scope of employment when torts occur on an employer's real property outside of business hours, the circumstances here are more analogous to Le Elder. Notably, no case cited by counsel or disclosed by the court's own independent research states or suggests that liability can be imposed on an entity like Lyft based on a "the business is the car" rationale. Le Elder has been cited approvingly numerous times and most recently in 2013 and has never been criticized or overruled, remains good law, and is on-point authority requiring the granting of this motion. The "coming and going" rule does not apply because defendant Jonathan Gaurano was returning home from working at a conference unrelated to Lyft when the accident happened and had not activated the Lyft platform at any point that day. Because the coming and going rule is inapplicable, per Le Elder, this court may not employ a "benefit to the employer" analysis, which is urged by both plaintiffs. (Le Elder, 21 Cal.App.4th at 1608 ("The trial court mistakenly applied a 'benefit to the employer' analysis. It may have been referring to the benefit-to-the-employer exception to the 'coming and going rule, but that rule is inapt here.")(citation omitted)). As with the employee in Le Elder, Mr. Gaurano was engaged in a "purely personal" matter at the time the accident occurred. Mr. Gaurano was returning home to eat after working at a conference unrelated to Lyft and the employee in Le Elder was returning home to eat after dropping his kids off at school. (Id.) The primary consideration is not whether the vehicle can be fairly considered a company vehicle or whether Lyft benefitted from Mr. Gaurano driving it for personal reasons, but whether Mr. Gaurano was acting within the scope of the alleged employment, agency agreement, or joint venture when the accident happened. (Id. at 1609 ("The existence of nonexistence of employer benefits is not dispositive in determining vicarious liability. Rather, the dispositive factor is whether an employee was acting within the scope of employment when the tort was committed.") (citation omitted)). None of the cases relied on by the plaintiffs are to the contrary. Lazar v. Thermal Equip. Corp. (1983) 148 Cal.App.3d 458, 463, 465-67 applied an exception to the coming and going rule applied because the driver's deviation from the scope of employment was minimal. Sumrall v. Modern Alloys, Inc. (2017) 10 Cal.App.5th 961 reversed a trial court's grant of summary judgment because it was a question of fact whether the driver was on a "business errand" when the accident occurred. (end of part 1 of 1) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: SABRINA MAREZ VS. LYFT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Lyft, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication As To Plaintiff Marissa Cruz'S Complaint. (For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 10, 2018, Line 4, Defendant Lyft, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication As To Plaintiff Marissa Cruz'S Complaint. (part 2 of 2) (For Tentative Ruling Only) Unlike the driver in Lobo v. Tamco (2010) 182 Cal.App.4th 297, Mr. Gaurano was not on call for customer requests and could not have been unless he activated the Lyft platform. (Lobo, 182 Cal.App.4th at 301-03 (holding that because the driver drove his vehicle home with an expectation that he might need to respond in-person to customer complaints, the driver was still acting within the scope of employment and vicarious liability was appropriate)). While no published California decision has addressed whether the Express Drive rental program is a sufficient ground to impose liability against Lyft when the liability event did not occur at a time when a driver was not on the Lyft platform, there is nothing about the Express Drive program that requires a different result than Le Elder reached with regard to the facts in that case where the driver was on call 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, and was reimbursed for various vehicle maintenance expenses. Just as Le Elder rejected a "totality of the circumstances argument, plaintiffs' efforts to create a triable dispute by focusing on the Express Drive program must fail. (Le Elder, 21 Cal.App.4th at 1609 ("The totality of the circumstances is of no legal significance where, as here, an employee is engaged in purely personal activity when his or her tort causes injury to a third party.")). Similarly, although Mr. Gaurano's misunderstanding regarding the extent of his insurance coverage in the Express Drive rental program may be attributable to Lyft's lack of clarity, that is not a basis for imposing vicarious liability against Lyft for an accident that occurred outside Mr. Gaurano's relationship with Lyft. Because the undisputed facts show that Mr. Gaurano acted outside the scope of his relationship with Lyft when the accident occurred, as a matter of law Lyft is not vicariously liable for Mr. Gaurano's alleged negligence under an employment, agency, or joint venture theory. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Lyft is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: OOLONG, LLC. VS. PETER ZUCCARELLI AND VACUUM COATING SUPPLIES, LLC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 10, 2018, Line 7, DEFENDANT PETER ZUCCARELLI, VACUUM COATING SUPPLIES LLC's Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint. Defendants Peter Zuccarelli and Vacuum Coating Supplies, LLC's ("VCS") motion to quash is granted. After review of the supplemental papers filed by the parties, the court remains of the view that Shisler v. Sanfer Sports Cars, Inc. (2006) 146 Cal.App.4th 1254 controls this case and requires granting of defendants' motion. Plaintiff Oolong, LLC has the burden of demonstrating sufficient minimum contacts to warrant the exercise of general or specific jurisdiction. Oolong's evidence fails to show jurisdiction over VCS or Mr. Zuccarelli, who are both located in Texas. The evidence establishes that VCS passively advertised the Leybold Syrus 1105 machine for sale on e-Bay, Oolong reached out to VCS to purchase the machine, Oolong prepared its own purchase invoice and sent it to VCS in Texas, and the parties subsequently had communications regarding modifying the machine. There is no salient difference between this action and Shisler. When VCS initially posted the advertisement on e-Bay it was not directly targeted towards California. This case, like Shisler, involved a one-time transaction, albeit there were many communications about the transaction. The fact that the parties subsequently had numerous communications regarding the modifications to the machine does not change the character of the transaction from a one-off deal. In sum, Oolong has no evidence to establish general jurisdiction and fails to establish the "purposeful availment" prong of the test for specific jurisdiction. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ANH NGUYEN VS. PAUL J. BURNS ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 10, 2018, Line 8, DEFENDANT UNION PACIFIC RAILROAD COMPANY, RUBEN BAUTISTA, MICHAEL GRIFFIN, ALBERTO FERNANDEZ's Motion To Amend Answer. Defendants Union Pacific Railroad Company, Ruben Bautista, Michael Griffin, and Alberto Fernandez's motion to amend answer is granted. The amended answer must be filed by December 20, 2018. Granting the motion comports with California's policy of liberally allowing amendments to pleadings, especially answers, at any stage of the case. Plaintiffs have not shown that they will suffer legal prejudice by the granting of the motion. rulings.Case Title: CLOVIS SILVA VS. ELAINE SHAW ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Denying In Part Plaintiff'S Motion To Quash Subpoenas
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, December 10, 2018, Line 1, PLAINTIFF CLOVIS SILVA's Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Denying In Part Plaintiff'S Motion To Quash Subpoenas. Pro Tem Judge Jeff Wohl, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion for reconsideration is denied for two reasons. First, the motion is untimely. Although the parties disagree whether the motion was due on November 5 or 6, 2018, it is undisputed that on November 5, 2018, plaintiff filed only a notice of motion, and that the balance of the papers, including the supporting declarations, were not filed until November 8, 2018. That renders the motion untimely. Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1008(a); Branner v. Regents of University of California, 175 Cal. App. 4th 1043, 1045 (2009). Second, even if the motion was timely, it does not show good cause for reconsideration. The supporting declaration must show "what application was made before, when and to what judge, what order or decisions were made, and what new or different facts, circumstances, or law are claimed to be shown. Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1008(a). Plaintiff previously advanced the same argument why the subpoenas should be quashed as he does here, and he does not satisfactorily establish why he could not have introduced the San declaration in support of his original motion to quash the subpoenas. Motions for reconsideration are appropriate only in extraordinary circumstances. New York Times Co. v. Superior Court, 135 Cal.App.4th 206, 212-13 (2005). This is not one of them. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jeffwohl@paulhastings.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: RUSSELL STANALAND DOING BUSINESS AS STANALAND & VS. THE LAMB & FRISCHER LAW FIRM , LLP, A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT The Lamb And Frischer Law Firm Llps Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 10, 2018, Line 9, DEFENDANT THE LAMB & FRISCHER LAW FIRM's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Motion For Summary Adjudication. Defendant The Lamb & Frischer Law Firm LLC's motion for summary adjudication as to the second, third and fourth causes of action for conversion, constructive trust and fraud in the complaint filed by plaintiff Russell Stanaland is denied as to all three causes of action. The motion is denied as moot as to the conversion and constructive trust claims because Mr. Stanaland dismissed those claims. The motion is denied as to the fraud claim because L&F failed to maintain its summary motion burden of production. Mr. Stanaland's fraud theory is that once L&F learned that the settlement offers were not memorialized, which was after the parties entered into their agreement, L&F concealed the fact that L&F intended to assert that the agreement was invalid when it received the settlement funds. (Complaint 3:10-5:16; 7:1-24.) L&F's facts 44-56 fail to show that Mr. Stanaland's fraud theory cannot be established. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Stanaland is required to prepare a proposed order in compliance with CCP 437c(g) which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)
